title:
Clinical Scanning
Workflow
subtitle: Redesigning the clinical scanning workflow for speed, clarity, and decision-making in real treatment conditions.

slides.title:
Clinical Scanning
Workflow
slides.description:
The patient is in the chair. The scanner is ready. The system isn't.
iTero is a digital intraoral scanner used chairside during live patient appointments. I redesigned Align Technology's RX, Scan, and View workflow into one continuous system.

slides.label: The System
slides.title: A system used during live patient treatment
slides.content: iTero is used chair-side. Three phases — RX, Scan, View — feed into each other. A problem in one disrupts the whole appointment.
issues: RX → define patient, procedure, case details
Scan → capture a real-time 3D model
View → validate before lab submission
slides.highlight: Every decision happens under time pressure — with the patient in the chair.

slides.label: The Problem
slides.title: The Rx was a form. The clinic isn't.
slides.content: Sequential, popup-heavy, and rigid — it forced clinical decisions before clinicians had the information to make them.
issues: Every tooth, material, and spec triggered a modal
No multi-select — procedures set tooth by tooth
Changing a procedure meant starting over
Setup had to finish before scanning could begin
slides.highlight: Every popup happened with a patient watching.

slides.label: Who It Affected
slides.title: The problem reached further than the chair
slides.description: Setup failures didn't stop at the clinician. Every incomplete field and blocking modal sent ripple effects downstream.

issues.title: Dentists
issues.description: Every extra click was time not spent on the patient — setup friction happened chair-side, with the patient watching.

issues.title: Clinical staff
issues.description: Incomplete cases and mandatory-field errors generated resubmissions — administrative work created by avoidable UI gaps.

issues.title: Labs
issues.description: Received Rx data that was incomplete or unstructured for complex procedures like All-on-X and multi-phase treatments.

slides.label: Why Now
slides.title: Three signals converged at once
slides.content: 3Shape, Medit, and Shining3D had already modernised their Rx flows. The iTero architecture had been patched too many times to patch again — the only path forward was a rebuild.
issues: Competitors had modernised — iTero's Rx flow was visibly behind
At this scale, every extra popup per case was expensive
The codebase couldn't absorb another patch — only a rebuild would hold
slides.highlight: At 37 million cases a year, one unnecessary popup per case adds up fast.

slides.label: Research & Discovery
slides.title: What I heard in the room
slides.content: I interviewed doctors across procedure types — restorative specialists, general dentists, orthodontists. I asked each one to walk me through a real case: where they slowed down, what they wished worked differently. Five themes came back in every session.

quotes.text: It feels like a long boring form — and I'm doing it with a patient in the chair.
quotes.author: Restorative Specialist

quotes.text: I change the procedure multiple times before I even begin scanning.
quotes.author: General Dentist

quotes.text: There's always another popup. I just want to get to the scan.
quotes.author: Orthodontist

quotes.text: Every time I select a tooth, something opens. I can't see what I've already done.
quotes.author: Restorative Specialist

quotes.text: The moment I go looking for a tool, I lose my rhythm completely.
quotes.author: General Dentist

slides.label: User Flow Mapping
slides.title: What clinicians actually do — not what the product assumed
slides.content: The research surfaced what hurt. Mapping the flow showed where each complaint lived — and the friction clustered at the handoffs between phases.
issues: RX → Scan: setup blocked the clinician before any scan began
Scan → View: tool access broke the clinician's rhythm mid-capture
View → submit: no completion signal — View felt unfinished
slides.issuesTitle: Three breakpoints — one per phase handoff
slides.highlight: The interviews told me what hurt. The flow told me where to fix it.

image.caption: Appointment flow map — three handoffs, three breakpoints

slides.label: Design Goals
slides.title: Goals by phase

goals.title: Setup runs alongside the scan
goals.description: Rx happens in parallel, not before.

goals.title: Configuration in one step
goals.description: Multi-select teeth, group materials, one interaction.

goals.title: Scanning stays uninterrupted
goals.description: Tools accessible without leaving the capture.

goals.title: One visual language
goals.description: No icon memorisation, no mixed styles.

kpis: Time to find the right tool

slides.label: Constraints
slides.title: What I kept in mind while designing

issues.title: Three surfaces
issues.description: Scanner (touch-only, gloved hands), MIDC desktop, and web — hover works with a mouse, but the scanner can't rely on it.

issues.title: Backward compatibility
issues.description: Old Rx stayed live for in-progress cases.

issues.title: Feature-flagged
issues.description: On or off per company — learnable on first contact, every time.

issues.title: Read and demo states
issues.description: Three states designed in from day one — not retrofitted.

description: Not every constraint forced a specific decision — but each one stayed in the back of my mind while designing, shaping what felt safe to ship across all three surfaces.

slides.label: Interactive Prototype
slides.title: A coded prototype to test decisions before development
slides.content: Figma shows what a screen looks like. It can't show how the workflow actually feels — the rhythm, the motion, the real environment. I built the prototype in code so timing and flow problems surfaced before any screen was locked.
bullets2: Flow problems caught before any screen was locked
Timing and motion testable, not just static frames
A decision made in the morning, tested the same afternoon
slides.highlight: Some design decisions can only be made in motion.

slides.title: RX
slides.subtitle: Defining the patient, procedure, and case before scanning — and the setup gate I had to clear first.

slides.label: RX Setup — Before
slides.title: Setup blocked the scan before it could start
slides.content: The old product was a dense, multi-step form — patient and procedure gated behind blocking modals before scanning could begin. Every change opened another popup.
issues: Patient selection required a multi-step modal
Changing a procedure triggered a blocking popup every time
Dropdown disconnected from the dental chart — no visual link to teeth
Procedure had to be defined upfront, before any scan data existed
slides.highlight: Every second here was a second the patient sat waiting.
slides.caption: Old RX setup — the blocking multi-step form.

slides.label: RX Setup — Exploration
slides.title: Three approaches — tested with clinicians on real cases
slides.dir1Desc: Step-by-step wizard. Organised, but clinicians felt railroaded — too rigid for chairside use.
slides.dir2Desc: Collapse/expand layout — tap to open, read, close, repeat. In practice, clinicians reopened the same section constantly.
slides.dir3Desc: Everything visible at once — no steps, no walls. Same fields, same order as before — just nothing in the way.

slides.label: RX Setup — After
slides.title: Setup runs alongside the scan — not before it
slides.content: Same fields, same order — just no walls between them. The setup is shorter, the menus aren't buried, and the actions that mattered most are visible at a glance.
issues: Single view — no modals, no popups, less scrolling
Change procedure easily — pick or swap in one tap
New patient created from a dedicated, always-visible entry
slides.caption: New RX setup — single-view, no blocking form, much less scrolling.

slides.label: RX Tooth Selection
slides.title: Selecting teeth should be as fast as pointing at them
slides.beforeDescription: Every tooth needed its own modal — click, confirm, close, repeat. No overview of what you'd already done.
slides.afterDescription: Teeth selected directly on the chart — one or many at once — with procedures assigned inline.

slides.title: Scan & View
slides.subtitle: Capture and validation treated as one continuous phase — the clinician never leaves the screen between them.

slides.label: Scan Toolbar — Before
slides.title: Tools scattered, with no home
slides.content: Icons built up from different eras, different styles, no shared visual language — and no consistent place to find them mid-scan.
issues: No shared visual language across icons
No dedicated place for tools
Every new feature added more scatter
slides.highlight: A clinician shouldn't have to find a tool — the tool should find them.
slides.caption: Old toolbar — scattered, mixed-style icons.

slides.question: How can every tool live in one place — without ever getting in the doctor's way?
slides.support: A single home tools could scale into, predictable enough that clinicians never had to look for it — and never had to fight it mid-scan.

slides.label: Looking Outward
slides.title: How other platforms solved the same problem
slides.content: Instead of inventing something new, I looked at how other software handles tools that need to live in one place. Every platform with a lot of tools used the same pattern — a toolbar.
issues: One dedicated location for tools
A pattern that scales as new tools get added
Out of the way of the work, but always findable
slides.highlight: The format wasn't a guess — it's how platforms with lots of tools have always solved this.

slides.label: Toolbar — Exploration
slides.title: Once the toolbar was the answer, the question became where to put it
slides.dir1Desc: Bottom center. Sat across the occlusal area — and some clinicians had to stretch to reach it depending on how they were positioned.
slides.dir2Desc: Vertical right rail. Always visible, but it overlapped and competed with other tools — interfering with controls clinicians already used.
slides.dir3Desc: Top-right corner. The only position clinicians could reach without pulling their eyes off the model — out of the scan area, out of the way.

slides.label: Scan Toolbar — After
slides.title: One toolbar, one visual language
slides.content: A top-right toolbar — out of the scan area, always findable. Icons rebuilt as one system: consistent line weight, consistent style, labeled.
issues: Toolbar fixed to one corner — always findable
Unified icon set — each tool reads at a glance
New tools slot in without requiring new conventions
bullets2: Toolbar and procedure icons rebuilt as one visual system
Consistent line weight and style across every tool
slides.highlight: Placement and language solved together — not in sequence.
slides.caption: New top-right toolbar + unified icon set.

slides.label: Multi-scan & Compare
slides.title: Managing multiple scans and comparing them
slides.content: Complex cases need pre-treatment, treatment, and additional reference scans. Tabs scaled with the case and clinicians already knew the pattern. Overlay with adjustable opacity shows reduction prep, fit, and alignment side by side.
bullets2: Tabs scale with the case — a pattern clinicians already know
Overlay scans with adjustable opacity — see reduction prep, fit, and alignment
What clinicians used to hold in memory, now visible on screen

slides.label: View Tools
slides.title: From icon bars to clinical decision panels
slides.beforeDescription: Unlabelled icon clusters with no hierarchy — every meaning memorised, nothing self-explanatory.
slides.afterDescription: Named panels with one clear primary action — the same structure across every tool, nothing to memorise.

slides.label: Design System
slides.title: Every component built in three states from the start
slides.content: Every pattern from the redesign became a reusable component — built in three states (editable, read-only, demo) and pushed to Storybook so engineering had a single source of truth.
slides.highlight: The three-state architecture became the shared language the rest of the product was built on.

image.caption: Design system components formalised from the redesign

slides.label: Outcomes
slides.title: Five goals, five outcomes — what changed in measurable terms

outcomes.title: Setup no longer gates the scan
outcomes.description: Patient and procedure fill in alongside the scan — no mandatory step before capture.

outcomes.title: Multi-tooth selection in one step
outcomes.description: Select a range, apply procedure and material in one interaction.

outcomes.title: Scrolling and buried menus
outcomes.description: Shorter setup view — procedure, patient, and teeth all swappable in seconds.

outcomes.title: Scanning stays in rhythm
outcomes.description: Toolbar fixed in one corner — tools reachable without leaving the scan.

outcomes.title: One visual language across every tool
outcomes.description: Unified icon set replaced scattered styles — each tool reads at a glance.

slides.label: Reflection
slides.title: What I took away
whatWorked: Building in Claude Code with engineers — one shared artifact, no handoff.
Hands-on development — building parts of the UI myself meant faster rollout and quicker UX QA.
Borrowing proven patterns — toolbar and tabs from products clinicians already use. Familiar over novel.
Cross-functional negotiation — technical discussions with engineering shaped scope without degrading the experience.
whatFailed: Jumped into design before fully learning the clinical world — had to go back to clinicians for terms and daily flow I missed.
Showed the new icons individually before showing the full toolbar — clinicians rejected them in isolation, only accepted the system once they saw it together.
Tooth chart took multiple rounds — making it scalable for complex cases while keeping the familiar context took longer than scoped.
whatYoudDoDifferently: Spend more time with clinicians before opening Figma — understand the world first, design second.
Validate systems in context, not in isolation — show the whole pattern, not the parts.
Add an AI assistant to the RX — drafting notes and procedure suggestions as the clinician works.
slides.whatYouCouldntMeasure: Outcomes were validated in prototype testing, not the field. Post-launch metrics still pending.
slides.whatYouLearned: Getting the diagnosis right before Figma shaped everything — and choosing familiar patterns made the rest of the work cheaper to ship.

slides.title: Thank You
slides.subtitle: The Rx looked like a form problem. It was an architecture problem.
